title: Prepare for Class Assignments
section: Class #2: Comparing Articles: Breiman vs Shmueli
text:
Class #2: Comparing Articles: Breiman vs Shmueli
Breiman, Leo: Statistical Modeling: The Two Cultures
Main takeaways:

There are two cultures in statistics: data modeling and algorithmic modeling.
Data models dominate but often:

Assume the wrong structure
Give fragile conclusions
Hide poor predictive performance.

Predictive accuracy is the most honest test of a model.
Many different models can fit the same data equally well - Rashomon effect.
Simplicity and accuracy are in conflict - Occam dilemma
High dimensionality can help, not hurt - Bellman’s reversal
Algorithmic models:

Often predict better and can provide more reliable information

He argues that statistics should focus on solving problems, not defending models.
The future of statistics depends on embracing different types of tools

Shmueli, Galit: To Explain or to Predict?

Main takeaways:

Statistical modeling is used for causal explanation, prediction, and description.
Many fields conflate explanation and prediction, assuming models with high explanatory power automatically has high predictive power - this would be wrong and harmful to scientific progress.
Explanatory modeling and predictive modeling serve different scientific goals and affect every step of the modeling process.
Focuses on explanatory modeling (causal explanation) vs. predictive modeling (empirical prediction)

How the two articles compare:
Breiman argues that statistics should prioritize prediction accuracy and real-world performance over strict probabilistic modeling assumptions.

Sees explanatory models as often weak predictors.
Argues that prediction is undervalued in classical statistics.
Promotes models like random forests and other black-box methods.

Shmueli argues that confusion in statistics arises because explanation (inference) and prediction are fundamentally different goals that require different methods and evaluation criteria.

Explanatory modeling→ causality, theory testing
Predictive modeling→ forecasting, decision support
Warns against using inferential tools (p-values, coefficients) for predictive claims.

How are they similar?:
Both criticize traditional statistical practice for conflating explanation and prediction.
How are they different?:

Breiman pushes statistics for prediction and algorithms.
Shmueli pushes statistics toward clarity and goal alignment.

title: Prepare for Class Assignments
text:
Class #4: Summarize “Mining Scientific Papers”
i. “Mining Scientific Papers: NLP-enhanced Bibliometrics”

Traditional bibliometrics relies mainly on metadata, but new methods analyze full-text scientific papers.
NLP and machine learning allow deeper analysis of citation context, argument structure, and scientific writing.
The growth of open access datasets and NLP tools enables large-scale mining of research literature.
Combining bibliometrics + NLP + computational linguistics helps reveal patterns in how knowledge is produced and cited.
Future research focuses on better datasets, citation analysis, and automated knowledge extraction from scientific papers.

ii. “Mining Scientific Papers, Volume II: Knowledge Discovert and Data Exploitation”

Scientific literature is growing rapidly, making automated analysis essential.
Text mining, NLP, and machine learning allow researchers to analyze large collections of papers.
These techniques help identify research trends, citation networks, and knowledge structures.
The field relies heavily on open-access datasets and large research corpora.
Combining bibliometrics with computational methods enables deeper insights into how scientific knowledge develops.

Discussion questions based on “Mining Scientific Paper”

Why is it useful to use NLP and machine learning to analyze scientific papers?
How can analyzing the full text of research papers help researchers understand scientific trends better than just looking at citations?
What are some benefits of using automated tools to study large collections of scientific articles?

Class #6: Association Rules and Proposal Presentation
What are Association Rules? These are a data mining method use to find relationships or patterns between items in a data set.
Whats the difference between Data Mining and Unsupervised Learning? Data mining is a broad process of discovering useful patterns, trends, or knowledge from large datasets. Unsupervised learning is a type of machine learning where the algorithm learns patterns without labeled data.

title: Prepare for Class Assignments
text:
Class #7: Cross Validation + Big Data
What is cross-validation?: Cross-validation is a method used to evaluate how well a model will perform on new, unseen data. Instead of testing the model on the same data used to train it, the data is split into multiple parts so the model is tested on data it has not seen before.
How to sample big data? Why is it important? What are the difficulties? How to solve the problem?:
Sampling means selecting a smaller subset of data from a very large data set so analysis becomes manageable. Common sampling methods:
1. Random Sampling Every observation has equal probability.
2. Stratified Sampling Data is divided into groups (strata), then sampled from each group.
3. Systematic Sampling Select every kth observation.
4. Cluster Sampling Divide data into clusters and sample entire clusters.
Why is this important? Big data sets often have millions or billions of observations, high computational cost, long processing time, and memory limitations

title: Prepare for Class Assignments
text:
Class 8: NLP and LLM’s
Do a quick survey based on the readings how NLP and LLM have advanced Knowledge Mining as a field.
a. Summarize the findings:
The readings show that the central finding is that LLMs represent a paradigm shift in NLP that fundamentally expands the scope of knowledge mining. LLMs are trained on massive corpora using self-supervised learning. Knowledge is implicitly stored in model parameters and this enables generalization across all tasks and reduced need for task specific models.
There are also pre-trained models that improved language understanding and transfer learning. Knowledge mining becomes more semantic and less dependent on manual preprocessing. LLMs require new evaluation frameworks and traditional metrics are insufficient. Challenges include hallucination, reasoning reliability, and benchmark limitations.
b. Identify the key concepts:
Pretraining and scaling laws, in-context learning, transfer learning, and self-attention and transformer architecture.

title: EPPS 6302 - Assignment 1 + DataFramed Podcast Review
text:
Hello!

This website has been created using RStudio Quarto. I have chosen the theme called “Journal”, which is simple yet eye catching.
The navigation bar is organized between personal and educational tabs. I have included my information about myself, research projects, and my resume. The rest is organized by my previous courses that required the use of a Quarto website.
Thank you!

DataFramed Podcast Review: 
Can we Create an AI Doctor? with Aldo Faisal, Professor in AI & Neuroscience at Imperial College
This podcast was presented by Dr. Aldo Faisal and discussed the role of AI in healthcare. Key takeaways from this podcast were:

Utilize federated machine learning to train AI models on sensitive healthcare data while preserving patient privacy, enabling the development of robust AI systems without compromising data security.
Investigate the potential of ambient intelligence in healthcare, which automates operational tasks like transcribing doctor-patient conversations and updating patient records, to reduce administrative burdens and enhance clinical decision-making.
Consider the development of large health models, akin to large language models, that can process multimodal data from electronic health records, medical literature, and research data to provide comprehensive insights into patient care and treatment outcomes (DataCamp2025).


Review:
Reading the title of the podcast, what immediately came to mind was an AI robot doctor that you would see on TV. This is what Dr. Faisal mentioned in the beginning of the podcast, that this is not to be mistaken with just that. Using AI in the medical field can help doctors and nurses treat patients faster and more accurately. An example of how AI has helped within the medical space has been regarding diagnostics. According to Dr. Faisal, licenses for these are already in the process of being licensed and used in the workspace. AI can be used to help tumor/shadow detection on scans, transcribe a conversation between a doctor and patient, identify symptoms, make suggestions on medication, and prefill information to make the doctor’s clerical load lighter. 
Nightingale AI is the name of Dr. Faisal’s project. Based in the UK, this is a large health model trained in electronic health records, imaging, labs, genetics, pathology, telemetry, and literature. The purpose is to have support provided for healthcare providers. Their main concern was how to obtain large amounts of medical data, which includes cooperating with healthcare providers and patients, and what to do with the data. It was mentioned that patients were concerned regarding their privacy and for their data to not be abused. However, Dr. Faisal stated that the main idea is to create an AI that learns about medicine in a different way that other large language models are applied to. They want to build models that are trained on multimodal data, so that the model is able to ingest, understand, and reason information the same way a medical doctor would reason science. 

Example given of how Nightingale AI will help doctors in the future: 

Ex. The patient has a chest infection and the doctor will prescribe a certain type of penicillin. The system then can predict how the patient’s xray will look depending on what type of medicine that is prescribed to the patient. 
Ex. The patient has liver issues and the doctor prescribes a drug to cure another issue in the kidney. How will this medication affect your liver and what are all of the possible side effects and how do all of these drugs interact with each other? What are all the positive and negative side effects? 
Polypharmacy problem - this is what pharma researchers want to know when testing a medicine and which populations they can test on without issues. 
Ex. Can be used to understand how a patient evolves - the system has a “digital twin” which includes a description of your body’s physiology. This would be useful to see how an asthmatic patient would do in an environment that is hot, polluted, and can potentially save many lives

Dr Faisal mentioned something that stuck with me. Concerning patients and their data privacy, he mentioned that sick patients almost always give their data away. They want to give their data to get better and to help others who are sick as well. The aim of the healthy is to not give their data away. There are different laws that protect people and their data, as well as how it can be used by insurers or employers. Data leakage is a possible risk however they are considering that as well. 
Overall, this particular podcast gave some amazing insights into the use of AI within the healthcare sector. I am not familiar with the healthcare world, so this was very insightful and allowed me to understand more of how AI can be used around the world. 

DataCamp. 2025. “Can We Create an AI Doctor? with Aldo Faisal, Professor in AI & Neuroscience at Imperial College.” DataFramed (podcast episode), July 28. DataCamp. https://www.datacamp.com/podcast/can-we-create-an-ai-doctor

title: EPPS 6323 - Assignment 2

Objective: Design a prompt to conduct a structured systematic literature review on data mining and machine learning using multiple AI models.
Models in Use: ChatGPT Plus, Copilot, & Grok.

title: EPPS 6323 - Assignment 2
text:
Step 1: Initial Prompt Creation + Input
Prompt: “Conduct a 150 word structured systematic literature review on the applications of data mining and machine learning in real-world domains. Include the methodology section, synthesize key findings, identify trends and gaps, and propose a one testable hypothesis. Use an academic tone and emulate systematic review standards”.

title: EPPS 6323 - Assignment 2
text:
Step 2: Analyze Model Responses
ChatGPT:
Objective: Summarize real-world applications of data mining and machine learning (ML) across domains.
Methods: Using PRISMA-style screening, we searched IEEE Xplore, ACM DL, PubMed, and Scopus (2015–2025) for (“data mining” OR “machine learning”) AND (healthcare OR finance OR manufacturing OR transportation OR agriculture). Included were peer-reviewed empirical studies reporting deployment or field evaluation; excluded were purely methodological papers. Title/abstract then full-text screening was applied; evidence was narratively synthesized by domain and task.
Findings: Supervised learning dominates (risk scoring, defect detection, demand forecasting); unsupervised methods support anomaly/fraud detection and segmentation. Benefits include higher accuracy, faster decision cycles, and cost reduction, but external validity is limited by single-site datasets.
Trends and gaps: Deep learning and real-time/edge analytics are rising; gaps persist in monitoring, fairness auditing, interpretability, and prospective trials.
Testable hypothesis: In high-stakes workflows, interpretable ML with calibrated uncertainty increases adoption and reduces errors versus opaque models, measurably in routine operational settings.
Analysis: Chat delivered on their results! However, compared to Copilot and Grok, Chat did not do the best job. The language was fairly academic and more vague compared to the other models.

Copilot:
Methodology: A systematic search of recent peer‑reviewed articles and reviews on data mining and machine learning (ML) applications across domains such as healthcare, finance, cybersecurity, education, transportation, and e‑commerce was conducted using scholarly databases. Inclusion criteria focused on empirical or review studies reporting real‑world deployments or large‑scale evaluations of ML or data mining techniques.
Synthesis of key findings: Across domains, supervised learning (e.g., SVMs, neural networks) dominates tasks such as diagnosis, fraud detection, demand forecasting, and intrusion detection, while clustering and association mining support segmentation and pattern discovery. Studies consistently report performance gains over rule‑based or statistical baselines, especially with high‑volume, heterogeneous data. Springer
Trends and gaps: Emerging trends include hybrid models, integration with IoT streams, and domain‑specific optimization. Persistent gaps involve limited external validation, scarce interpretability analyses, and underreporting of fairness, privacy, and deployment costs. msijr.msi-ggsip.org
Testable hypothesis: In real‑world healthcare prediction tasks, interpretable ML models with domain‑informed features achieve non‑inferior accuracy compared with black‑box deep models while yielding higher clinician trust.
Analysis: Copilot delivered on their results! More academic on their tone compared to ChatGPT. However, I personally believe Grok delivered better results.

Grok:
Data mining and machine learning (ML) have transformed real-world domains by enabling predictive analytics, pattern discovery, and automated decision-making from large datasets.
Methodology: This mini-systematic review synthesizes evidence from prominent reviews and studies (primarily 2020–2025), drawing on sources identified through web searches for systematic literature reviews and application-focused papers on ML/data mining in domains like healthcare, agriculture, finance, and industry. Inclusion focused on high-impact works discussing real-world applicability, trends, and challenges, emulating PRISMA-inspired selection for recency and relevance.
Key Findings: ML applications dominate healthcare (e.g., disease prediction using random forests, logistic regression, and support vector machines on real-world data for oncology/neurology prognosis), finance (fraud detection, risk assessment), agriculture (precision farming, yield prediction, pest/disease management via deep learning), manufacturing (predictive maintenance, quality control), and others like cybersecurity and smart cities. Supervised techniques (e.g., neural networks, decision trees) prevail, with deep learning enhancing image/video analysis.
Trends and Gaps Trends: include integration with big data/IoT for sustainable practices and real-time processing, alongside growing emphasis on explainable AI. Gaps persist in external validation (<1% in some healthcare reviews), limited longitudinal/open datasets, underrepresentation of basic/lower-education contexts or non-Western domains, ethical/security concerns, and rare adoption of advanced reinforcement learning in complex real-world scaling.
Testable Hypothesis: Hypothesis: Models incorporating explainable AI techniques (e.g., SHAP values) will achieve higher adoption rates in regulated domains like healthcare compared to black-box deep learning models, as measured by deployment frequency in clinical trials or production systems over the next five years.
(Word count: 150)
Analysis: Grok delivered on their results! The language used was very academic compared to the previous models.

title: EPPS 6323 - Assignment 2
text:
Step 3: Refine the Prompt
ChatGPT:
Conduct a 150 word structured systematic literature review on the applications of data mining and machine learning in real-world domains. Include the methodology section, synthesize key findings, identify trends and gaps, and propose a one testable hypothesis. Use a formal academic tone and avoid vague generalizations. Include clearly labeled sections for Methodology, Key Findings, Trends and Gaps, and Testable Hypothesis. Be specific about the domains discussed, such as healthcare, finance, manufacturing, and education, and emphasize concrete examples of applications. Ensure the methodology follows a PRISMA-style review format and that the hypothesis is precise, testable, and relevant to current research.

Copilot:
Conduct a 150 word structured systematic literature review on the applications of data mining and machine learning in real-world domains. Include the methodology section, synthesize key findings, identify trends and gaps, and propose a one testable hypothesis. Use an academic tone and systematic review structure with sections for Methodology, Synthesis of Key Findings, Trends and Gaps, and Testable Hypothesis. Focus on peer-reviewed studies and ensure that all claims are grounded in credible academic literature rather than broad or unclear references. Highlight not only dominant methods and applications but also explain how trends and research gaps connect to future scholarly directions.

Grok:
Conduct a 150 word structured systematic literature review on the applications of data mining and machine learning in real-world domains. Include the methodology section, synthesize key findings, identify trends and gaps, and propose a one testable hypothesis. Maintain a rigorous academic tone and include clearly labeled sections for Methodology, Key Findings, Trends and Gaps, and Testable Hypothesis. Strengthen the methodology by explicitly describing database selection, inclusion criteria, and review process in a more formal systematic review style. Keep the strong domain examples and detailed insights, but ensure the response is concise, structured, and methodologically rigorous.

title: EPPS 6323 - Assignment 2
text:
Step 4: Cross-Model Collaboration
Task: Integrate the best elements from each model’s output into a final systematic review. Write a new prompt to synthesize the results.
Final Review of Chosen Model: CoPilot
Methodology: Peer‑reviewed empirical and systematic reviews on data mining and machine learning (ML) in real‑world settings were identified through database searches following PRISMA principles, emphasizing implemented or clinically embedded systems rather than proof‑of‑concept models.
Synthesis of key findings: Studies report dominant use of supervised learning (neural networks, support vector machines, random forests) for prediction, diagnosis, and prognosis, especially in hospital‑based healthcare analytics and deep‑learning–driven imaging workflows. Implementations show improved decision support, workflow efficiency, and patient outcomes, but often with limited external validation and sparse reporting of performance metrics.Nature+1
Trends and gaps: Trends include rapid growth of ML deployments post‑2018, concentration in oncology, neurology, and ophthalmology, and focus on clinical prediction rather than prescriptive or organizational analytics. Major gaps concern real‑world implementation science (costs, sustainability, stakeholder acceptability), integration of domain expertise, and underrepresentation of non‑healthcare sectors in prospective studies.pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov+2 These gaps point toward interdisciplinary work combining ML, implementation frameworks, and socio‑technical evaluation.
Testable hypothesis: In prospective real‑world deployments, ML systems co‑designed with end‑users and evaluated using hybrid effectiveness‑implementation designs will demonstrate higher sustained adoption than systems developed without structured implementation frameworks.

title: EPPS 6323 - Assignment 2
text:
Step 5: Reflection
Each model had different strengths.
ChatGPT had clear structure and a PRISMA-style methodology, but its synthesis was more general and less detailed. Copilot produced a more polished academic tone with concise summaries, though it relied on broader references and weaker connections between findings and research gaps. Grok stood out for its depth, offering detailed domain-specific examples and stronger discussion of trends and gaps, but its methodology was less formally structured and systematic. 
The most effective prompt refinements were those that targeted each model’s weaknesses.
For ChatGPT, adding instructions to reduce vagueness and include specific examples improved precision. For Copilot, emphasizing credible sourcing and clearer links between findings and implications strengthened its analytical depth. For Grok, refining the methodology section to be more formal and systematic improved its overall academic rigor while maintaining its strong insights.
Overall, this exercise showed that AI models are not a one and done thing!
They require tailored prompting to achieve the best results. Effective prompts must be iterative and model-specific, especially for structured academic tasks like systematic reviews. Leveraging multiple models and synthesizing their strengths can significantly improve the quality, depth, and reliability of academic writing.

title: Assignment 4
text:
Read Quanteda tutorial: https://tutorials.quanteda.io/
Analyze:
a. US presidential inaugural speeches and any similarities or differences over time and among presidents?

        
The speeches are similar because many presidents use different themes such as nation, people, freedom, government, and unity.
The differences appear over time because earlier speeches often focus more on constitutional government, union, and public duty, while modern speeches tend to include more references to global issues, democracy, security, and social change.

b. US Department of Defense reports (USDOD.zip)

What is wordfish? Wordfish is a text scaling model used to estimate the position of documents based on word usage. It assumed the documents with similar word patterns are closer together, while documents with different patterns are set apart.
a. Run Wordfish on DOD reports. The Wordfish model estimates the relative position of each DOD report based on word usage. Reports placed closer together are likely to use similar language and emphasize similar policy concerns. Reports farther apart likely differ in terminology.

       

How to compare positions: They can be compared by looking at the Wordfish theta scores. Documents with higher/lower theta values are interpreted as being located at different positions on the estimated text dimension.

title: Assignment 4
text:
Weak entity set: one whose existence that is dependent on another entity set, called its identifying entity set instead of associating a primary key with a weak entity. We use the PK of the identifying entity along with discriminator attributes to uniquely identify a weak entity
Strong entity set: the opposite of a weak entity set. It has a PK and does not depend on any other entity for its existence.

Ex. Hospital Management System:
Patient Table in a Hospital Management System (Strong Entity) is the primary key because each patient has a unique Patient ID, it does not depend on another entity.
Weak entities:
Prescription: A prescription is given to a patient by a doctor relies on the doctor and cannot exist without the doctor as it depends on the Patient ID and Doctor ID.
Surgery: Different patients might have the same surgery, so it depends on the patient ID and Doctor ID.

Conducted qualitative research through interviews, literature review and coding of transcripts.
Collected, analyzed, and synthesized qualitative data to draw conclusions.

title: Weekly Reflections
text:
February 10th: Cathy O’Neil: Weapons of Math Destruction
Weapons of Math Destruction

AI and originality

O’Neils argument is that AI and algorithimc systems are not original and replicate and amplify existing human bias despite being thought as being objective and neutral.

Algorithms are built from historical data, which can be unequal, have discrimination bias, and flawed ideas.
They are pattern enforcers
AI automates old assumptions and gives them legitimacy.

AI is being too heavily relied on, and too trusted!

What is AGI? How does AGI affect scientific research?

AGI is defined as Artificial General Intelligence: this is a form of AI that can reason across domains, being flexible to learn, and can apply intelligence broadly. It affects scientific research because it can add faster data analysis and pattern detection, automation of repetitive tasks, and give insights at a large scale. However, it can reduce research quality, have hidden bias, and a loss of interpretability.

February 17th: AI
How to start using AI for our project?

We will be using AI as a tool in our project. We will be using it to aid in the brainstorming, creation, and implementation of our research project. The whole exchange will be documented and disclosed at the end of the project.

Which two AI models will we be using? and how to leverage them to help your research?

Within our team members, we have access to ChatGPT and Gemini. We will use them to brainstorm and use as a tool for our research project.


February 24th: Humans and Knowledge
a. I believe that humans find knowledge in many different ways. Whether it is through pure curiosity and researching, or simply through life experience, it is something that happens on an everyday basis. Children learn in school everyday, but it would not be until you apply it to real life situations is where that information becomes knowledge. What we choose to do with the information is what knowledge becomes. For example, I can read the guidebook to how to use R/RStudio all that I want. However, I will not know how to apply that information until I am getting hands on use with R to really learn it.
b. AI discrepancies/failures.

High profile cases where AI was utilized as a therapist/mental health resource.
AI may be bias, as it is only knowledgeable as the information it has been trained on
May generate errors that are outdated or misinformation
Cheating - students can use AI to write essays and complete assignments. AI is a tool and should be utilized as a tool instead of a replacement.


March 3rd: Machine Learning Pipeline
a. Starting a Machine Learning pipeline! What is a Machine Learning pipeline? This is an automated, end to end workflow that connects every step of the machine learning life cycle.
b. What did the error analysis reveal that the accuracy score alone did not? How does over fitting relate to knowledge vs noise?

March 10th: Prediction vs Explanation
a. Prediction vs Explanation:
Prediction is what will happen. A guess or hypothesis on outcome will happen.
Explanation is why something happened. The reasoning and scientific research behind it.
b. Simple Causal Diagram for relationship in our research area:
“Do Major U.S. news outlets differ in how they frame the ICE related fatal shootings during Operation Metro Surge (Jan 2026)?”
Media Outlet Ideology -> Framing of the Event -> Emotional Language -> Political Context -> Audience
Possible Cofounders (a variable that influences both the media outlet and the framing): Political Context, Audience Ideology, or Event Details

March 24th: Text Mining, NLP, and LLMs

How can text mining reveal patterns that close reading alone cannot? What are its blind spots?

Text Mining can analyze thousands and millions of documents simultaneously. It can also detect macro level patterns (such as trends over time and across outlets). It also detects patterns and gives consistent and you are able to replicate the results. Blind spots are loss of context, oversimplifying complex narratives, model bias, casualty limitations, and important outliers may be dismissed.

How NLP and LLM can assist in your research and provide data/assistance for your project? Give specific examples.

NLP and LLM’s can assist in data collection and processing. NLP tools can use API’s, scraping, and cleaning. It can also help with topic modeling. LLM’s can help with qualitative coding and to classify frames, summarize articles, and generate coding schemes.

Name limitations and suggest solutions:

Limitations include but are not limited to: - Bias in models and data: this skews results. Solution: Use diverse datasets and validate with human coding - Hallucination (LLMs): False insights. Solution: Cross check with original text - Over-Automation: Loss of critical thinking. Solution: Combine with close readings.

Write an AI for research guide (you can use bullet points) incorporating ethical, technical and academic concerns that could lead to more problems than solutions.

Ethical Considerations: - Bias and fairness, data ethics, and misuse risk
Academic Integrity: - Do not treat AI outputs as facts, always double check. - Use AI as a tool, not as an author - Disclose if AI is being used
Workflow:
1. Collect data
2. Clean and preprocess (NLP)
3. Explore patterns (topics, sentiments)
4. Use LLM for coding assistance and summarization
5. Validate with close readings
6. Interpret with theory

March 31st: LLM’s Pros and Cons

What can LLM’s do well and where do they fail? Evaluate one specific failure mode (hallucination, bias, reasoning error) someone may encounter.

What LLM’s Do Well - They excel at condensing massive data sets, long documents, or messy meeting transcripts into concise bullet points.

LLM’s are highly proficient in structured logic, helping developers write boilerplate code or find syntax errors in seconds.
They offer dozens of variations for headlines, emails, or marketing copy.
Beyond literal translation, they can rewrite a technical manual to sound like a children’s book or turn a formal email into a casual text.
A hallucination occurs when a model generates a response that is grammatically perfect and highly confident but factually incorrect.

The Reasoning: LLM’s do not have a built-in “truth” compass. They operate on probabilistic associations. If you ask a model about a fictional historical event, it may “hallucinate” a detailed timeline because its training data contains millions of examples of what a “historical timeline” looks like. It fills the structural template with plausible-sounding but invented data.
Example: In a legal setting, a lawyer once used an LLM to find precedents. The model provided six citations that looked perfectly legitimate, complete with case numbers and judge names. However, none of those cases actually existed; the model had simply synthesized the pattern of a legal citation.

How might generative AI reshape our discipline in the next 5 years and what research tasks might change the most and what should remain human driven.

Gen AI will reshape data analysis in many different ways. I believe that research tasks will be likely to change, with AI handling the initial sweep of papers and locate data quicker and more efficient. It would save time for researchers with tasks that would initially take weeks to do. It would also help with data cleaning and formatting. The thing that should remain human driven is the use of ethics. Researchers should use ethical scoping to decidenext steps and use their best judgment.

April 7th: What is RAG?

What is Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) and why does it matter for research? How does grounding generation in retrieved documents change the trustworthiness of AI output?

RAG is an approach that combines document retrieval with language generation, where an AI first pulls relevant external sources (like articles or data sets) and then generates responses grounded in that evidence. This matters for research because it improves trustworthiness by making outputs more verifiable, transparent, and up-to-date, reducing hallucinations compared to standalone models.

What are the recent criticisms about RAG? Any suggestions for improving generation?

RAG relies heavily on the quality of retrieval—if the wrong or biased documents are retrieved, the output will reflect those errors and for sometimes producing shallow analysis rather than true synthesis. Current improvements focus on better retrieval methods, multi-step reasoning, and stronger prompting to ensure the model accurately uses and cites the retrieved information.

April 14th: Knowledge Graphs

What is a knowledge graph and how does it differ from a traditional database or a vector store? Why are relationships between entities valuable for knowledge mining?

A knowledge graph is a structured representation of information where entities are connected by relationships, forming a network of meaning rather than just stored records. Unlike a traditional database, a knowledge graph explicitly encodes relationships between entities, allowing systems to reason over connections. These relationships are valuable for knowledge mining because they enable more complex queries uncover hidden patterns, and support explainable insights rather than just retrieving similar data points.

Identify three entities and three relationships from your research domain that could form a small knowledge graph. How would this structure help answer questions that a flat dataset cannot?

We are researching media framing of ICE-related shootings. Three entities could be 1. News outlet 2. Frame type (e.g., humanitarian, law-and-order) 3. incident
Three relationships could be:

“Covers” (news outlet → incident)
“Uses frame” (news outlet → frame type)
“Describes” (frame type → incident)

This structure can allow us to questions like: “Which outlets consistently use law-and-order framing for specific incidents?” “How do framing patterns differ across outlets for the same event?”
This is something a flat dataset cannot easily capture because it lacks explicit connections and multi-level relationships between variables.

title: EPPS 6354 Assignment 6
text:
a. JSON - Tik Tok
Tik Tok uses JSON for data exchange between servers and users. Using JSON is efficient for the social media platform because it transmits data quickly and allows it to be well structured for efficient processing. It organizes the data into different categories, such as videos, likes, and comments etc. that is needed to make Tik Tok an enjoyable experience. It also allows users to share across different platforms!
Structure and Composition of Tik Tok :

Front-end (User Experience): The purpose is to provide a fast and smooth engaging user experience. Built with HTML, CSS, and JavaScript.
Ex: FYP page and interactions
Back-end (Processing): The purpose is to manage user interactions, store videos, and real-time updates. Built with Python and Java.
Ex: User information, content delivery, and security and data encryption
Database System (Data Storage): The purpose is to store user data, videos, engagement metrics, and recommendation data.

Main Database Technologies used:

SQL/Postgre SQL for structured data (user accounts, interactions). Relational Databases
NoSQL - for handling highspeed and unstructured data

b. XML - CNN
CNN uses XML for the distribution, storage, and sharing of news and updates. This allows for a quick and efficient data transfer to allow for more information to get across to the world.
Structure and Composition of CNN:

News Updates: Provides news feeds in XML format, allowing for users to obtain real time news updates.
Storing News Content: XML databases allows news websites to store articles and content. This makes retrieval of information efficient.
Data Exchange: This allows for CNN to share its content across different platforms!

Main Database Technologies used:

SQL/Postgre SQL for structured data (user accounts, interactions). Relational Databases
NoSQL - for handling highspeed and unstructured data
XML Databases - for storing news articles, headlines, and metadata

section: Question 2 - Express the following query in SQL using no subqueries and no set operations using outer join:
text:
Question 2 - Express the following query in SQL using no subqueries and no set operations using outer join:
SELECT student.ID
FROM student
LEFT OUTER JOIN advisor ON student.ID = advisor.s_id
WHERE advisor.s_id IS NULL

Left Outer Join includes all students even though they dont have a match in advisor.
The (WHERE advisor.s_id IS NULL) filters out students who do have an advisor, leaving only those who dont.

title: EPPS 6354 Assignment 5
text:
Disconnected - at least one entity set that has no relationships with any other entity sets. This suggests that some parts of the database are isolated and do not interact with others.
Ex. 2 separate entity sets:
Students (ID, Name, Age)
Products (Product_ID, Name, Price)
If there is no relationship between them, then they are disconnected. This might indicate missing relationships in the design, or could be intentional or a design flaw

Cycle - means there is a loop in the entity relationships. This implies that a sequence of relationships leads back to the original entity

Ex.
Employee -> works for -> department
Dept -> managed by -> manager (also employee)
Manager -> reports to -> senior employee
Senior Employee -> works for -> department

forming a circle

Cycles may be essential in hierarchical structures, such as employee-manager relationships. However, unintended cycles can lead to data redundancy or infinite loops in queries.

Summary:
Disconnected graph: some entities are isolated (meaning missing relationships)
Cycle in graph: entities are interconnected in a loop (could be intentional or a design issue)

title: EPPS 6354 Assignment 5
section: Question 2 - Why do we still have weak entity sets?
text:
Question 2 - Why do we still have weak entity sets?
Weak entity sets naturally model real work scenarios where certain entities do not have an independent existence. They preserve the natural relationship in the data and reinforce constraints that ensure their existence depends on a related strong entity. This helps maintain data consistency.
Ex. A dependent must always be associated with an employee, ensuring meaningful relationships in the database.
Real-world representation: some concepts naturally fit in the weak entity model better

title: EPPS 6354 Assignment 5
text:
Question 3 - SQL Exercise
i. Find the ID and name of each employee who lives in the same city as the location of the company for which the employee works:
SELECT e.ID, e.person_name FROM employee AS e JOIN works AS w ON e.ID = w.ID JOIN company AS c ON w.company_name = c.company_name WHERE e.city = c.city;

Join employee with works using ID to get the company they work for
Join works with company using company name to get the companies city
Filter employees whos city matches the companies city


ii. Find ID and name of each employee who lives in the same city and on the same street as does her or his manager.
SELECT e.ID, e.person_nam FROM employee AS e JOIN manages AS m ON e.ID = m.ID JOIN employee AS mgr ON m.manager_id = mgr.ID WHERE e.city = mgr.city AND e.street = mgr.street;

Self join employee to compare an employee (e) with manager
Use manages to get the manager id for each employee
Filter employees who share the same city and street as their manager


iii. Find ID and name of each employee who earns more than the average salary of all employees of her or his company.
SELECT e.ID, e.person_name FROM employee AS e JOIN works AS w ON e.ID = w.ID WHERE w.salary > ( SELECT AVG(w2.salary) FROM works AS w2 WHERE w.company_name = w2.company_name)

Join employee with works to get salary information
Compare each employees salary to the average salary of all employees working in the same company


b) Consider the query… what is wrong with it?:
Using natural join can cause incorrect joins. Natural join joines tables based on common column names automatically If tables share multiple common columns, the database might join them incorrectly Could lead to wrong or missing results

Ambiguous Column Names Semester and year columns are likely in the section table, but the query does not specify The database might get confused if other tables also have the columns
Missing Explicit Conditions Instructor table has an ID column, but teaches also has ID Need to explicitly specify how tables are related using ON conditions

In short, the issue was due to the natural join making unintended matches, and the fixed query uses join… on to explicitly define relationships, preventing errors.

title: DataFramed Podcast Reviews
text:
This podcast was presented by Dr. Aldo Faisal and discussed the role of AI in healthcare. Key takeaways from this podcast were:

Utilize federated machine learning to train AI models on sensitive healthcare data while preserving patient privacy, enabling the development of robust AI systems without compromising data security.
Investigate the potential of ambient intelligence in healthcare, which automates operational tasks like transcribing doctor-patient conversations and updating patient records, to reduce administrative burdens and enhance clinical decision-making.
Consider the development of large health models, akin to large language models, that can process multimodal data from electronic health records, medical literature, and research data to provide comprehensive insights into patient care and treatment outcomes.

Reading the title of the podcast, what immediately came to mind was an AI robot doctor that you would see on TV. This is what Dr. Faisal mentioned in the beginning of the podcast, that this is not to be mistaken with just that. Using AI in the medical field can help doctors and nurses treat patients faster and more accurately. An example of how AI has helped within the medical space has been regarding diagnostics. According to Dr. Faisal, licenses for these are already in the process of being licensed and used in the workspace. AI can be used to help tumor/shadow detection on scans, transcribe a conversation between a doctor and patient, identify symptoms, make suggestions on medication, and prefill information to make the doctor’s clerical load lighter. 
Nightingale AI is the name of Dr. Faisal’s project. Based in the UK, this is a large health model trained in electronic health records, imaging, labs, genetics, pathology, telemetry, and literature. The purpose is to have support provided for healthcare providers. Their main concern was how to obtain large amounts of medical data, which includes cooperating with healthcare providers and patients, and what to do with the data. It was mentioned that patients were concerned regarding their privacy and for their data to not be abused. However, Dr. Faisal stated that the main idea is to create an AI that learns about medicine in a different way that other large language models are applied to. They want to build models that are trained on multimodal data, so that the model is able to ingest, understand, and reason information the same way a medical doctor would reason science. 
Example given of how Nightingale AI will help doctors in the future: 

Ex. The patient has a chest infection and the doctor will prescribe a certain type of penicillin. The system then can predict how the patient’s xray will look depending on what type of medicine that is prescribed to the patient. 
Ex. The patient has liver issues and the doctor prescribes a drug to cure another issue in the kidney. How will this medication affect your liver and what are all of the possible side effects and how do all of these drugs interact with each other? What are all the positive and negative side effects? 
Polypharmacy problem - this is what pharma researchers want to know when testing a medicine and which populations they can test on without issues. 
Ex. Can be used to understand how a patient evolves - the system has a “digital twin” which includes a description of your body’s physiology. This would be useful to see how an asthmatic patient would do in an environment that is hot, polluted, and can potentially save many lives


Nightingale AI is intended for use by AI, digital health, and clinical researchers so it can be focused on user experience and interface. Once the system evolves with a proper chat conversation prompt, it can be expanded for use within the general population. Dr Faisal mentioned something that stuck with me. Concerning patients and their data privacy, he mentioned that sick patients almost always give their data away. They want to give their data to get better and to help others who are sick as well. The aim of the healthy is to not give their data away. There are different laws that protect people and their data, as well as how it can be used by insurers or employers. Data leakage is a possible risk however they are considering that as well. 
Overall, this particular podcast gave some amazing insights into the use of AI within the healthcare sector. I am not familiar with the healthcare world, so this was very insightful and allowed me to understand more of how AI can be used around the world.

title: Assignment 3
text:
Review ISLR Chapter 3
Chapter 3 introduces linear regression, a simple but foundational method for predicting a quantitative response. Though it may seem basic compared to newer techniques, understanding it well is essential before moving on to more advanced models.
Run Lab_ML01.R:

       

Use TEDS2016 data set to run a multiple regression model. Access the data set using the following codes:


library(haven)
library(haven)

TEDS_2016 <- read_stata("https://github.com/datageneration/home/blob/master/DataProgramming/data/TEDS_2016.dta?raw=true")


Write a function called regplot to plot a regression line.


regplot <- function(x, y){
     fit <- lm(y ~ x)
     plot(x, y)
     abline(fit, col = "red")
}
regplot(TEDS_2016$age, TEDS_2016$PartyID)







regplot(TEDS_2016$edu, TEDS_2016$PartyID)







regplot(TEDS_2016$income, TEDS_2016$PartyID)









The regression plots created from the codes above :
a. Age: 
b. Education: 
c. Income: 
What is the problem and why?
The issue is that multiple regression and regression plots work best when the dependent variable is continuous. The dependent variable is categorical, not continuous. A categorical dependent variable does not meet the assumption of a continuous outcome. Due to this, the model can produce inaccurate or unrealistic predictions.
What can be done to improve prediction of the dependent variable?
To improve the prediction, a model designed for categorical outcomes should be used. If the dependent variable is a binary number, the logistic regression would be more appropriate. If it has more than two categories, a multinomial or ordinal logistic regression would be a better fit.

3. What are the differences between the two methods?

The pros are that there is no coding required and it is a quick export to CSV using the Google Trends Website. However, it is less flexible and it is not easily automated or able to repeat your queries.
Using gtrendsR in R, it is fully programmble which means there is reporoducible research. You can automate between multiple quieries and it is easy to save. However, you will need to know how to use R which everyone can learn!
